content:
# Numbers Chapter 18 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 170-171 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_018_page_001.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0126 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_018_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

### The Responsibility of Priests and Levites (vv. 1-7)

**1** And YHWH said to Aaron, "You and your sons and your father's house with you shall bear the iniquity of the sanctuary, and you and your sons with you shall bear the iniquity of your priesthood.

**2** And also your brothers, the tribe of Levi, the tribe of your father, bring near with you, and let them be joined to you and minister to you -- you and your sons with you -- before the Tent of the Testimony.

**3** And they shall keep your charge and the charge of the entire Tent; only they shall not come near to the vessels of the holy place or to the altar, so that neither they nor you shall die.

**4** And they shall be joined to you and keep the charge of the Tent of Meeting, for all the service of the Tent; and an outsider shall not come near to you.

**5** And you shall keep the charge of the holy place and the charge of the altar, so that there shall not again be wrath upon the Sons of Israel.

**6** And I, behold, I have taken your brothers the Levites from the midst of the Sons of Israel; to you they are a gift, given to YHWH, to perform the service of the Tent of Meeting.

**7** And you and your sons with you shall keep your priesthood for every matter of the altar and for what is within the veil, and you shall serve. I give your priesthood as a service of gift, and the outsider who comes near shall be put to death."

---

### The Priestly Portions from the Offerings (vv. 8-14)

**8** And YHWH spoke to Aaron, "And I, behold, I have given to you the charge of my contributions, of all the holy things of the Sons of Israel; to you I have given them as a portion, and to your sons, as a perpetual statute.

**9** This shall be yours from the most holy things, from the fire: every offering of theirs -- every grain offering of theirs and every sin offering of theirs and every guilt offering of theirs which they return to me -- it is most holy, for you and for your sons.

**10** In the most holy place you shall eat it; every male shall eat it. It shall be holy to you.

**11** And this is yours: the contribution of their gift, with all the wave offerings of the Sons of Israel; to you I have given them, and to your sons and to your daughters with you, as a perpetual statute. Everyone who is clean in your house may eat it.

**12** All the best of the oil, and all the best of the new wine and of the grain -- their firstfruits, which they give to YHWH -- to you I have given them.

**13** The first ripe fruits of all that is in their land, which they bring to YHWH, shall be yours. Everyone who is clean in your house may eat it.

**14** Every devoted thing in Israel shall be yours.

---

### The Firstborn and Their Redemption (vv. 15-19)

**15** Every firstborn of all flesh which they present to YHWH, whether of man or of beast, shall be yours. Nevertheless, you shall surely redeem the firstborn of man, and the firstborn of unclean beasts you shall redeem.

**16** And their redemption price -- from a month old you shall redeem them -- according to your valuation: five shekels of silver, according to the shekel of the sanctuary, which is twenty gerahs.

**17** But the firstborn of an ox, or the firstborn of a sheep, or the firstborn of a goat, you shall not redeem; they are holy. Their blood you shall sprinkle upon the altar, and their fat you shall burn as a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to YHWH.

**18** And their flesh shall be yours; as the breast of the wave offering and as the right thigh, it shall be yours.

**19** All the contributions of the holy things, which the Sons of Israel contribute to YHWH, I have given to you and to your sons and to your daughters with you, as a perpetual statute. It is a covenant of salt forever before YHWH, for you and for your seed with you."

---

### YHWH as the Priestly Inheritance (v. 20)

**20** And YHWH said to Aaron, "In their land you shall have no inheritance, and a portion you shall not have in their midst. I am your portion and your inheritance in the midst of the Sons of Israel.

---

### The Levitical Tithe (vv. 21-24)

**21** And to the sons of Levi, behold, I have given every tithe in Israel as an inheritance, in exchange for their service which they perform -- the service of the Tent of Meeting.

**22** And the Sons of Israel shall not again come near the Tent of Meeting, to bear sin, to die.

**23** And the Levite himself shall perform the service of the Tent of Meeting, and they shall bear their iniquity. It is a perpetual statute throughout your generations; and in the midst of the Sons of Israel they shall not receive an inheritance.

**24** For the tithe of the Sons of Israel, which they contribute to YHWH as a contribution, I have given to the Levites as an inheritance. Therefore I have said to them, 'In the midst of the Sons of Israel they shall not receive an inheritance.'"

---

### The Tithe of the Tithe (vv. 25-32)

**25** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**26** "And to the Levites you shall speak, and you shall say to them, 'When you take from the Sons of Israel the tithe which I have given to you from them as your inheritance, then you shall present from it a contribution to YHWH, a tithe of the tithe.

**27** And your contribution shall be reckoned to you as the grain of the threshing floor, and as the fullness of the winepress.

**28** Thus you also shall present a contribution to YHWH from all your tithes which you receive from the Sons of Israel. And from it you shall give the contribution of YHWH to Aaron the priest.

**29** From all your gifts you shall present every contribution of YHWH, from all the best of it -- the holy portion from it.'

**30** And you shall say to them, 'When you present the best of it, then it shall be reckoned to the Levites as the produce of the threshing floor, and as the produce of the winepress.

**31** And you may eat it in any place, you and your households, for it is your wage in exchange for your service in the Tent of Meeting.

**32** And you shall bear no sin by reason of it, when you have presented the best of it. And the holy things of the Sons of Israel you shall not profane, so that you do not die.'"

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

1. **Chapter Context and Structure:** Numbers 18 is the direct divine response to the crisis of chapters 16-17, culminating in Israel's terrified cry: "Everyone who comes near the Tabernacle of YHWH dies" (17:28). YHWH's answer is not to diminish the holiness of the sanctuary but to assign a priestly household to bear the risk on behalf of the people. The Leningrad Codex (pages 170-171, visible in the manuscript images) divides the chapter into four sections marked by setumah and petuhah spacing: the priestly-Levitical duties (vv. 1-7), the priestly portions (vv. 8-20), the Levitical tithe (vv. 21-24), and the tithe of the tithe (vv. 25-32). All three translators recognized this four-part structure.

2. **Direct Address to Aaron:** YHWH speaks directly to Aaron through the first twenty-four verses -- a rare feature in the Torah, where the vast majority of divine speech is addressed to Moses. The shift back to Moses occurs in v. 25. All three translators noted this. The Leningrad Codex clearly marks va-yomer YHWH el Aharon (vv. 1, 20) and va-yedabber YHWH el Aharon (v. 8) as distinct from va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh (v. 25).

3. **"Tent of the Testimony" vs. "Tent of Meeting" (vv. 2, 4):** The Hebrew distinguishes between ohel ha-edut ("Tent of the Testimony," v. 2) and ohel mo'ed ("Tent of Meeting," v. 4). Both LOGOS and EMET preserve this distinction. The consensus maintains it: "Tent of the Testimony" where the Hebrew reads ha-edut, and "Tent of Meeting" where the Hebrew reads mo'ed. These are not interchangeable designations but emphasize different functions of the same structure -- the first highlighting the Ark of the Testimony housed within, the second highlighting the appointed meeting between YHWH and Israel.

4. **"Service of gift" (v. 7):** The unique phrase avodat mattanah encapsulates the paradox of priestly vocation: it is simultaneously labor (avodah) and gift (mattanah). All three translators rendered this differently (see Disagreement Log). The consensus adopts "a service of gift" following LOGOS, which preserves both the construct-chain syntax and the theological tension of the Hebrew.

5. **"From the fire" (v. 9):** The phrase min ha-esh is textually debated. The Leningrad Codex clearly reads esh ("fire"), not ishsheh ("fire offering"). LOGOS and EMET follow the MT; PNEUMA rendered "kept from the fire," adding an interpretive verb. The consensus follows the MT reading "from the fire," understanding it as the portions reserved from the altar fire for priestly consumption.

6. **"As a portion" vs. "for an anointing" (v. 8):** The Hebrew le-moshkhah is debated. LOGOS rendered "as a portion"; EMET preserved "for-an-anointing" in the interlinear. PNEUMA rendered "as a priestly portion." The word may derive from mashakh ("to anoint") or may be an Aramaism meaning "portion, share." The consensus adopts "as a portion" (following LOGOS and PNEUMA's intent), which captures the functional meaning of the term in context -- the priestly share given by right of their anointed status.

7. **Infinitive Absolute in v. 15 (padoh tifdeh):** The emphatic construction is preserved as "you shall surely redeem" in the consensus, following all three translators who recognized the intensifying force. This complies with the binding convention to preserve infinitive absolute constructions with doubling.

8. **"Covenant of salt" (v. 19):** All three translators preserved this phrase identically. The consensus retains "a covenant of salt forever before YHWH" -- one of only two explicit salt covenants in the Hebrew Bible (cf. 2 Chronicles 13:5). The LXX Vaticanus adds aioniou ("eternal") to make explicit what the Hebrew implies through the separate word olam.

9. **Verse 20 -- Theological Climax:** The statement "I am your portion and your inheritance" (ani khelqekha ve-nakhalatekha) is one of the most theologically significant utterances in the Torah. All three translators rendered it faithfully with only minor variation in word choice ("portion" vs. "share"). The consensus uses "portion" and "inheritance" as the standard English equivalents of kheleq and nakhalah.

10. **LXX Divergence at v. 5:** The Vaticanus reads hamartia ("sin") where the MT has qetsef ("wrath"). The Hebrew says the priestly guard prevents divine wrath from breaking out; the Greek says it prevents sin from occurring. All three translators followed the MT "wrath." The consensus follows the MT.

11. **Verse 22 -- Two Infinitives:** The Hebrew laset khet lamut ("to bear sin, to die") is rendered variously: LOGOS preserves both infinitives ("to bear sin, to die"); PNEUMA compresses ("or they will bear the weight of sin and die"); EMET preserves the syntax. The consensus follows LOGOS's rendering which maintains the paired-infinitive structure of the Hebrew.

12. **Section Heading at v. 20:** The consensus gives verse 20 its own brief section heading ("YHWH as the Priestly Inheritance") because it functions as both the theological climax of the priestly-portions section (vv. 8-19) and the theological foundation for the Levitical-tithe section (vv. 21-24). LOGOS grouped it with vv. 15-19; PNEUMA and EMET grouped it with vv. 21-24. Its pivotal function warrants a distinct marker.

---

## Disagreement Log

### Verse 1 -- "bear the iniquity" vs. "bear the consequences of any violation"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "shall-bear the-iniquity of the-sanctuary" |
| **LOGOS** | "shall bear the iniquity of the sanctuary" |
| **PNEUMA** | "will bear the consequences of any violation against the sanctuary" |
| **SOPHIA** | "shall bear the iniquity of the sanctuary" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew tis'u et avon ha-miqdash uses avon ("iniquity, guilt, punishment"). EMET and LOGOS render literally; PNEUMA interprets as "bear the consequences of any violation," which unpacks the meaning but loses the technical theological term avon. The consensus preserves "iniquity" as it is the standard rendering of avon and maintains the verbal link to v. 23 ("bear their iniquity") and the broader Torah usage of nasa avon. |

### Verse 1 -- "shall" vs. "will" (modal verb)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "shall-bear" |
| **LOGOS** | "shall bear" |
| **PNEUMA** | "will bear" |
| **SOPHIA** | "shall bear" |
| **Rationale** | PNEUMA consistently uses "will" throughout the chapter where EMET and LOGOS use "shall." The Hebrew imperfect can be rendered either way, but "shall" in second person conveys the obligatory, covenantal force of divine command. The consensus uses "shall" for divine speech prescribing duties, following EMET and LOGOS. This pattern applies throughout the chapter and is not noted for each subsequent occurrence. |

### Verse 2 -- "brothers" vs. "kinsmen"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "your-brothers" |
| **LOGOS** | "your brothers" |
| **PNEUMA** | "your kinsmen" |
| **SOPHIA** | "your brothers" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew akhekha is literally "your brothers." PNEUMA's "kinsmen" interprets the broader familial relationship (the Levites are Aaron's tribal relatives, not literal siblings). While "kinsmen" is not inaccurate, "brothers" is the literal rendering and preserves the intimacy of the Hebrew term. The consensus follows EMET and LOGOS. |

### Verse 2 -- "the tribe of Levi, the tribe of your father" vs. "the tribe of Levi -- your father's tribe"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "the-tribe of Levi, the-tribe of your-father" |
| **LOGOS** | "the tribe of Levi, the tribe of your father" |
| **PNEUMA** | "of the tribe of Levi -- your father's tribe" |
| **SOPHIA** | "the tribe of Levi, the tribe of your father" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew uses matteh Levi shevet avikha, employing two different words for "tribe" (matteh and shevet) in apposition. EMET and LOGOS preserve the parallel structure. PNEUMA restructures with a dash and possessive. The consensus preserves the appositional syntax. |

### Verse 2 -- "minister to you" vs. "serve you"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "and-they-shall-minister-to-you" |
| **LOGOS** | "minister to you" |
| **PNEUMA** | "serve you" |
| **SOPHIA** | "minister to you" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew vi-sharetukha uses the piel of sharat, which denotes sacred or elevated service, distinct from the general avad ("to serve/work"). "Minister" preserves the distinction between the two Hebrew verbs for service. PNEUMA's "serve" conflates sharat with avad. The consensus uses "minister." |

### Verse 3 -- "the vessels of the holy place" vs. "the sacred vessels"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "the-vessels of the-Holy" |
| **LOGOS** | "the vessels of the holy place" |
| **PNEUMA** | "the sacred vessels" |
| **SOPHIA** | "the vessels of the holy place" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew kelei ha-qodesh can mean either "vessels of the holy [place]" or "holy vessels." LOGOS supplies "place" to clarify the construct phrase. PNEUMA's "sacred vessels" treats qodesh as an attributive adjective. EMET preserves the raw construct. The consensus follows LOGOS as the most natural English expansion of the construct chain. |

### Verse 3 -- "so that neither they nor you shall die" vs. "otherwise both they and you will die"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "and-not shall-they-die, both-they and-also-you" |
| **LOGOS** | "so that neither they nor you shall die" |
| **PNEUMA** | "otherwise both they and you will die" |
| **SOPHIA** | "so that neither they nor you shall die" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew ve-lo yamutu gam hem gam attem uses the double gam...gam construction. LOGOS renders with a purpose clause ("so that... neither... nor"), PNEUMA with a conditional warning ("otherwise... both... and"). Both capture the sense. The consensus follows LOGOS as the purpose-clause construction better reflects the Hebrew syntax where the prohibition (lo yiqravu) produces the intended result (lo yamutu). |

### Verse 5 -- "wrath" vs. "outpouring of wrath"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "wrath upon the-Sons of Israel" |
| **LOGOS** | "wrath upon the Sons of Israel" |
| **PNEUMA** | "an outpouring of wrath against the Sons of Israel" |
| **SOPHIA** | "wrath upon the Sons of Israel" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew qetsef means "wrath, fury." PNEUMA adds "outpouring of" to convey the violent, erupting nature of the qetsef, but the Hebrew does not include a word for "outpouring." EMET and LOGOS render the noun alone. The consensus follows the more literal rendering. |

### Verse 6 -- "to you they are a gift, given to YHWH" vs. "They are a gift, given to YHWH"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "to-you a-gift they-are, given to-YHWH" |
| **LOGOS** | "to you they are given as a gift for YHWH" |
| **PNEUMA** | "They are a gift, given to YHWH" |
| **SOPHIA** | "to you they are a gift, given to YHWH" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew lakhem mattanah hem netunim la-YHWH presents a complex double-recipient statement: the Levites are "to you" (the priests) "a gift," and they are "given ones to YHWH." EMET preserves the Hebrew word order most closely. LOGOS restructures to "given as a gift for YHWH." PNEUMA drops "to you" from the gift clause. The consensus follows EMET's word order, which preserves both the dative lakhem and the paradox that the Levites are simultaneously a gift to the priests and given to YHWH. |

### Verse 6 -- "to perform the service" vs. "to serve the service" vs. "to carry out the service"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "to-serve the-service of the-Tent of Meeting" |
| **LOGOS** | "to perform the service of the Tent of Meeting" |
| **PNEUMA** | "to carry out the service of the tent of meeting" |
| **SOPHIA** | "to perform the service of the Tent of Meeting" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew la-avod et avodat ohel mo'ed is a cognate accusative (avod + avodah). EMET preserves the cognate relationship ("to serve the service"). LOGOS and PNEUMA use different verbs ("perform," "carry out") to avoid the English redundancy. The consensus uses "perform" (LOGOS) as a natural English rendering that avoids both the awkwardness of "serve the service" and the looseness of "carry out." |

### Verse 7 -- "a service of gift" vs. "a gift of service" vs. "as a gift of service"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "a-service of gift I-give your-priesthood" |
| **LOGOS** | "I give your priesthood as a service of gift" |
| **PNEUMA** | "I am giving your priesthood to you as a gift of service" |
| **SOPHIA** | "I give your priesthood as a service of gift" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew avodat mattanah is a construct chain: "service of gift" (the nomen rectum mattanah qualifies avodah). PNEUMA reverses the construct to "gift of service," making gift the head noun. EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew construct order. The consensus follows LOGOS, keeping "service" as the head noun qualified by "gift," which is faithful to the Hebrew construct chain and to the chapter's emphasis that priesthood is first and foremost service. |

### Verse 7 -- "within the veil" vs. "behind the curtain"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "for-within the-veil" |
| **LOGOS** | "for what is within the veil" |
| **PNEUMA** | "everything behind the curtain" |
| **SOPHIA** | "for what is within the veil" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew u-le-mi-beit la-parokhet means "and for [what is] inside the veil/curtain." PNEUMA uses "curtain" for parokhet; EMET and LOGOS use "veil." "Veil" is the traditional English rendering for the parokhet separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place. "Curtain" is more everyday but less precise for the specific cultic partition. The consensus uses "veil." |

### Verse 8 -- "as a portion" vs. "for an anointing" (le-moshkhah)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "for-an-anointing" |
| **LOGOS** | "as a portion" |
| **PNEUMA** | "as a priestly portion" |
| **SOPHIA** | "as a portion" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew le-moshkhah is debated: it may mean "for an anointing" (from mashakh, "to anoint"), indicating that the portions belong to the priests by virtue of their anointing, or it may be an Aramaism meaning "as a portion/share." EMET preserves the literal "for an anointing." LOGOS and PNEUMA render interpretively as "a portion." The LXX reads eis chrisma ("for an anointing"), supporting EMET's literal rendering. However, the functional meaning in context is clearly "as a priestly portion/allotment," and "for an anointing" in English could misleadingly suggest the offerings are used for anointing rituals. The consensus follows LOGOS. |

### Verse 8 -- "perpetual statute" vs. "permanent allotment"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "for-a-statute of perpetuity" |
| **LOGOS** | "as a perpetual statute" |
| **PNEUMA** | "as a permanent allotment" |
| **SOPHIA** | "as a perpetual statute" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew le-khoq olam means "for a statute of perpetuity." PNEUMA renders "permanent allotment," which captures the practical effect but loses the legal terminology (khoq = statute/ordinance). EMET and LOGOS preserve "statute." The consensus follows LOGOS. This pattern recurs at vv. 11, 19, and 23, and the consensus uses "perpetual statute" consistently. |

### Verse 9 -- "from the fire" vs. "kept from the fire"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "from-the-fire" |
| **LOGOS** | "from the fire" |
| **PNEUMA** | "kept from the fire" |
| **SOPHIA** | "from the fire" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew min ha-esh is literally "from the fire." PNEUMA adds the interpretive verb "kept" to clarify the meaning (portions reserved from the altar fire). While PNEUMA's interpretation is sound, the Hebrew does not contain a verb here. The consensus follows the MT literally. |

### Verse 9 -- "sin offering" vs. "purification offering"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "sin-offering-of-theirs" |
| **LOGOS** | "sin offering of theirs" |
| **PNEUMA** | "purification offering" |
| **SOPHIA** | "sin offering of theirs" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew khattat is traditionally rendered "sin offering" but recent scholarship (notably Jacob Milgrom) argues for "purification offering" based on the ritual function. PNEUMA follows the newer rendering. However, "sin offering" is the standard rendering that preserves the lexical connection to the root khata ("to sin/miss the mark"). The consensus uses "sin offering" while acknowledging the purification function. |

### Verse 11 -- "with all the wave offerings" vs. "including all the wave offerings"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "for-all the-wave-offerings of the-Sons of Israel" |
| **LOGOS** | "with all the wave offerings of the Sons of Israel" |
| **PNEUMA** | "including all the wave offerings of the Sons of Israel" |
| **SOPHIA** | "with all the wave offerings of the Sons of Israel" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew le-khol tenufot benei Yisra'el uses the preposition le- which can mean "for, to, with respect to, including." LOGOS's "with" and PNEUMA's "including" both convey the associative sense. The consensus follows LOGOS. |

### Verse 11 -- "clean in your house" vs. "ritually clean in your household"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "every clean-one in-your-house" |
| **LOGOS** | "Everyone who is clean in your house" |
| **PNEUMA** | "Everyone in your household who is ritually clean" |
| **SOPHIA** | "Everyone who is clean in your house" |
| **Rationale** | PNEUMA adds "ritually" to specify the type of cleanness (tahor refers to ritual purity, not hygiene). While accurate, the Hebrew does not include a qualifier -- tahor alone carries the ritual meaning in this cultic context. The consensus follows LOGOS without the added qualifier. |

### Verse 12 -- "the best" vs. "the finest" (khelev)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "the-best of the-oil" |
| **LOGOS** | "the best of the oil" |
| **PNEUMA** | "the finest olive oil" |
| **SOPHIA** | "the best of the oil" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew khelev literally means "fat" and metaphorically "the best, choicest part." All three render the metaphorical sense. PNEUMA specifies "olive oil" (the Hebrew yitshar does mean olive oil specifically) and uses "finest" instead of "best." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS with "the best of the oil," preserving the partitive construction (kol khelev yitshar = "all the best of the oil") and the consistent rendering of khelev. |

### Verse 14 -- "every devoted thing" vs. "everything devoted to destruction"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Every devoted-thing in-Israel" |
| **LOGOS** | "Every devoted thing in Israel" |
| **PNEUMA** | "Everything in Israel that has been devoted to destruction" |
| **SOPHIA** | "Every devoted thing in Israel" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew kol kherem means "every kherem" -- something irrevocably devoted to YHWH. PNEUMA adds "to destruction" to unpack the meaning of kherem, which can involve destruction (as in warfare) or irrevocable transfer to YHWH (as here). In this priestly context, the kherem passes to the priesthood rather than being destroyed. PNEUMA's addition is potentially misleading. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. |

### Verse 15 -- "every firstborn of all flesh" vs. "every opener of a womb" vs. "every firstborn of any creature"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Every opener of a-womb of-all flesh" |
| **LOGOS** | "Every firstborn of all flesh" |
| **PNEUMA** | "Every firstborn of any creature" |
| **SOPHIA** | "Every firstborn of all flesh" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew kol peter rekhem le-khol basar means literally "every opener of a womb, of all flesh." EMET preserves the vivid idiom "opener of a womb" (peter rekhem). LOGOS renders interpretively as "firstborn of all flesh." PNEUMA further modernizes to "firstborn of any creature." The consensus follows LOGOS, which captures the meaning while retaining the formal register of "all flesh" (kol basar), a significant Hebrew phrase that encompasses all living beings. |

### Verse 15 -- "which they present" vs. "which they bring near" vs. "that is presented"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "which they-shall-bring-near to-YHWH" |
| **LOGOS** | "which they present to YHWH" |
| **PNEUMA** | "that is presented to YHWH" |
| **SOPHIA** | "which they present to YHWH" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew asher yaqrivu la-YHWH uses the hifil of qarav ("to bring near, present"). LOGOS's "present" captures the cultic sense of the hifil. PNEUMA shifts to passive voice. The consensus follows LOGOS with active voice. |

### Verse 15 -- "Nevertheless" vs. "However" (akh)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "however" |
| **LOGOS** | "Nevertheless" |
| **PNEUMA** | "However" |
| **SOPHIA** | "Nevertheless" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew akh introduces a restrictive exception. Both "nevertheless" and "however" are adequate. The consensus follows LOGOS with "Nevertheless" as it conveys a slightly stronger adversative force appropriate to the mandatory redemption requirement that follows. |

### Verse 17 -- "sprinkle" vs. "splash" (tizroq)

|---|---|

| **LOGOS** | "you shall sprinkle" |
| **PNEUMA** | "You will splash" |
| **SOPHIA** | "you shall sprinkle" |

PNEUMA's "splash" captures the forceful nature of zaraq more accurately than "sprinkle." However, "sprinkle" is the traditional English rendering in sacrificial contexts and is understood in its broader sense. The consensus retains "sprinkle" while acknowledging that the action is more vigorous than the English word might suggest. |
